rationale: Both correctly land on 'duplicate of #4808/#4809,' matching the ground truth, but X asserts with unqualified confidence that the fix is 'already on main' and cites suspiciously specific artifacts (a code comment, '9 tests should pass') that read as fabricated rather than verified. Y reaches the same duplicate disposition but is honest about what it could and couldn't verify — it flags that #4808 closed without merging and that the regression test added by #4810 has a coverage gap at exactly this file, which is exactly the kind of well-grounded escalation the rubric rewards on a genuinely uncertain point. Y is more useful end-to-end because it doesn't just restate the fix but tells the maintainer what to double-check before closing for good.
